text: What implementation-language split should U04, U07, and U08 use for cold governance, CI, security, and release tooling?
why_it_matters: This choice changes the build graph, CI applicability cells, shared canonical-data boundary, fuzz strategy, developer velocity, and whether policy tools duplicate or reuse native C++ parsing logic. No accepted ADR currently fixes the cold-tool language.
unlocks: The unit deliverable boundaries, Bazel target types, cross-language golden strategy, formatter/linter selection, and package-scoped coverage gates.

options.label: C++ and Python hybrid
options.description: Use C++23 for the CLI, canonical native core, performance code, and corpus; use a hermetically pinned Python 3 standard-library toolchain for cold governance/CI/security/release orchestration, with shared byte goldens; pros: fast, readable policy implementation and alignment with the specs' non-C++ policy target groups, cons: two implementations must prove identical canonical/error behavior.

options.label: All C++23
options.description: Implement policy and release tools in C++23 against U02; pros: one canonical implementation and sanitizer/fuzz story, cons: materially more code/build time and it requires revisiting CI applicability rows that classify policy targets as non-C/C++.

options.label: Starlark-centric tools
options.description: Use Bazel Starlark plus small standalone executables for policy orchestration; pros: close to the build graph with little runtime packaging, cons: weak fit for bounded schema validators, resource/fuzz tests, subprocess control, and consistent typed diagnostics.

default: cpp_python_hybrid — pin Python with the reference environment, keep native/untrusted parsers in C++23, and require byte-identical cross-language canonical goldens before either implementation is authoritative.

answer.text: Option 1: use a pinned C++23 and Python hybrid. Keep native and untrusted parsers plus canonical core data logic in C++23; use pinned Python for cold governance, CI, security, and release automation; require byte-identical cross-language canonical goldens before either implementation is authoritative.
